skill.name: gh-cli
skill.description: Comprehensive GitHub CLI reference for managing repositories, issues, pull requests, Actions, releases, gists, codespaces, organizations, and authentication from the command line.
skill.summary: Use GitHub CLI commands for common GitHub development and operations workflows.
skill.author: github

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command execution, network, filesystem, token, and SSH-key patterns in SKILL.md. Review confirms these are documentation examples for GitHub CLI rather than hidden executable code or prompt injection, but several examples can alter local package configuration, GitHub resources, tokens, SSH keys, and repository data if run without approval.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Installation Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The Linux installation example downloads a GitHub CLI package keyring and writes apt sources using sudo. This is a legitimate installation pattern, but it changes system package trust and installs software if an agent runs it automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present and include sudo and network access. Context shows they are documentation examples, not hidden code, so the risk is execution-by-agent rather than malicious payload.

medium_findings.title: Token Handling Examples Can Expose GitHub Credentials
medium_findings.description: The skill shows setting GH_TOKEN directly and exporting the output of gh auth token. These examples support automation, but tokens in environment variables can be inherited by child processes or mishandled by logs and shell workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token references are explicit and semantically connected to authentication. The examples use placeholders or local gh output, so I did not find evidence of credential exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: State-Changing Bulk Operations Require Confirmation
medium_findings.description: The examples use gh list commands piped through xargs to close issues and edit pull requests in bulk. This is legitimate workflow automation, but it can modify many GitHub records if used without reviewing the selected items.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The xargs examples directly invoke state-changing gh commands. The surrounding context is workflow documentation, so the risk is accidental overbroad execution rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: SSH Key Path Findings Are Public-Key Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged SSH private key access, but the reviewed lines reference public key files ending in .pub for deploy keys and account SSH keys. The path is sensitive context, yet I did not find evidence that private key material is read or exfiltrated.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples use .pub files, which are public keys, and no network exfiltration of private key files appears in the reviewed context. The GPG example uses an odd SSH public-key path, but still not a private key.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Heuristics Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns across SKILL.md, but reviewed examples are GitHub CLI commands, flags, labels, descriptions, or markdown headings. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation or unsafe crypto API use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations show ordinary documentation text and CLI flags, not crypto code. Because only SKILL.md exists, there is no separate executable implementation where these crypto findings could apply.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: A targeted search for audit override phrases found no prompt-injection text. The only matched word was a normal command option description for custom repository override.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Keyword review did not identify instruction override language. This is lower confidence than a full semantic proof because the file is long, but sampled context supports benign documentation intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Download With Sudo Package Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The installation snippet fetches a package keyring over HTTPS and writes system apt configuration. Users should verify the source and avoid automated execution without approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pattern is present and has privileged local effects. It is a standard install example, which limits suspicion of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Workflow Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow examples assign command output into shell variables and environment variables. This is common CLI usage, but agents should not execute it until the target repository and token scope are confirmed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution syntax is explicit in the cited lines. Reviewed context indicates workflow automation rather than obfuscation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bulk xargs Mutation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples pipe issue and pull request numbers into xargs commands that close issues or add labels. This can have broad repository impact when filters are incorrect.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands perform repeated state changes through gh. Their intent is clear automation, but the operational risk is real.

content.value_statement: GitHub work often requires switching between web pages and terminal commands. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured GitHub CLI reference for practical repository workflows.
actual_capabilities: Provides command examples for gh authentication, account switching, and token-based automation.
Covers repository creation, cloning, browsing, syncing, deploy keys, labels, topics, and branch operations.
Documents issue and pull request workflows, including creation, review, merge, comments, labels, and bulk updates.
Explains GitHub Actions commands for runs, workflows, jobs, artifacts, caches, and secrets.
Includes references for releases, gists, codespaces, organizations, projects, aliases, extensions, and completions.
Shows JSON, jq, and template output patterns for scripting with gh command results.
limitations: It is a command reference, not a live wrapper around the GitHub API.
It does not verify repository names, permissions, tokens, or organization policies before commands run.
Some examples are state-changing and require human confirmation before execution.
Version-specific commands may differ from the installed gh version on a user system.

use_cases.title: Prepare Pull Request Workflows
use_cases.description: Find the right gh commands to create, review, merge, and monitor pull requests from the terminal.

use_cases.title: Automate Repository Maintenance
use_cases.description: Use issue, label, release, workflow, and repository commands to build repeatable maintenance scripts.

use_cases.title: Support GitHub Onboarding
use_cases.description: Give teammates a single reference for authentication, repository setup, codespaces, and common GitHub CLI tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Basic Command
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to use GitHub CLI for this task: [describe task]. Show the safest gh command and explain any required placeholders.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Repository Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a step-by-step gh workflow to create a repository named [name], add labels, set a description, and prepare the first pull request.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Risky Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this gh command before I run it: [paste command]. Identify state changes, token risks, required permissions, and safer dry-run checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Automation Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a gh-based automation plan for [workflow]. Include JSON field selection, jq filtering, confirmation gates, and rollback notes.

output_examples.input: User asks how to create a pull request from the current branch.
output_examples.output: The assistant gives a short gh pull request command, identifies title and body placeholders, and notes that the command creates a GitHub record.

output_examples.input: User asks for a safe way to close stale issues in bulk.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes listing matching issues first, reviewing the numbers, then running the close command only after explicit confirmation.

output_examples.input: User asks how to inspect a failing GitHub Actions run.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests commands to list runs, view the failed run, inspect jobs, and download logs without changing repository state.

best_practices: Confirm the target owner, repository, and host before running commands that create, delete, close, merge, or edit resources.
Use minimum required token scopes and avoid printing tokens in shared logs or transcripts.
List and review matching records before running bulk commands with xargs or filters.
anti_patterns: Do not run install commands with sudo from an AI suggestion without verifying the source and package configuration.
Do not paste real GitHub tokens into prompts, examples, issue comments, or shell history.
Do not run bulk issue, pull request, or repository commands before checking the selected items.

faq.question: Does this skill run GitHub CLI commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance. The user or agent environment decides whether commands are executed.

faq.question: Is this skill useful for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives these tools a structured reference for GitHub CLI workflows and command options.

faq.question: Can it manage pull requests and issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents gh commands for creating, viewing, editing, reviewing, merging, closing, and labeling GitHub records.

faq.question: Does it include GitHub Actions commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers workflow runs, jobs, logs, artifacts, caches, secrets, and related Actions commands.

faq.question: What permissions are required?
faq.answer: Permissions depend on the command. Authentication, repository access, organization rights, and token scopes must be checked first.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: Many examples are state-changing. Review targets and permissions before running commands that modify GitHub or local system state.
